ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'seafood salad' class (FOODON_03302114); no Thai-specific yam talay entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers seafood salad as a composite dish; no Thai-specific entry. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for Thai seafood salad as a prepared dish.

preparation_method: mixed with fresh herbs and chillies

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Thai seafood salad is approximately 250 g, typically presented as a shared or individual starter plate containing mixed seafood tossed in a spicy lime-fish sauce dressing with fresh herbs.

nutrient_source.title: Yam Talay (Thai Spicy Seafood Salad) — composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central entries for mixed seafood and Thai salad dressing components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for yam talay as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for: cooked shrimp (FDC 175180), cooked squid (FDC 175185), cooked mussels (FDC 175168), lime juice (FDC 167747), fish sauce (FDC 173268), and fresh herbs/chillies. Weighted proportions reflect a typical Thai seafood salad recipe (approx. 55% mixed seafood, 15% dressing, 20% vegetables/herbs, 10% shallots/aromatics). HPB Singapore Foodo and ICMR-NIN do not carry a direct yam talay entry. FAO/INFOODS SEA does not carry this specific composite. USDA SR Legacy used as the highest-priority available approved source for component-level estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed seafood salad and analogous low-carbohydrate seafood dishes — estimated from University of Sydney GI Database reference values for seafood and low-carbohydrate salad composites
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for yam talay in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 38 based on analogous low-carbohydrate seafood salad composites. Seafood proteins and lime-based dressings contribute negligible glycemic load; the primary carbohydrate sources are small amounts of shallots and natural sugars in the dressing. The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI values for seafood dishes in the range 30–45; 38 is used as a conservative mid-range estimate. GL per serving = (38 × 5.8 g carbohydrate per 100g × 250g serving / 100) / 100 ≈ 5.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a 250 g serving of Thai seafood salad provides approximately 1300 mg sodium, primarily from fish sauce, which represents more than half the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced fish sauce or substituting with low-sodium soy sauce can meaningfully lower the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Thai seafood salad has a low glycemic index (estimated 38) and a low glycemic load per serving (approximately 5.5), making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when fish sauce quantity is moderated to control sodium intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium from fish sauce; beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from seafood
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The mixed seafood in yam talay provides beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and lean protein with low saturated fat, which are cardioprotective; however, the high sodium content from fish sauce warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease, and portion control or reduced-sodium preparation is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Seafood Salad, known in Thai as ยำทะเล (Yam Talay), is a classic Thai dish consisting of mixed seafood — typically shrimp, squid, and mussels — that are briefly blanched or lightly cooked and then tossed in a bold, tangy dressing made from fresh lime juice, fish sauce, fresh chillies, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, shallots, garlic, and fresh herbs such as mint and coriander. The dish is served at room temperature or slightly chilled and is characterised by a vibrant interplay of sour, spicy, salty, and umami flavours with a light, refreshing texture. It is a staple of Thai cuisine and is widely served across Thailand and in Thai restaurants globally, including in Singapore where it appears on menus such as Saap Saap Thai. Nutritionally, yam talay is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate, low-fat dish with approximately 82 kcal per 100 g, making it one of the lighter Thai restaurant options. The seafood provides lean protein (approximately 10.2 g per 100 g), omega-3 fatty acids, vitamin B12, selenium, and zinc. The primary nutritional concern is sodium, which is elevated due to fish sauce and can reach approximately 1300 mg per standard 250 g serving. The glycemic index is low (estimated 38), making it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. It is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycemic load, but sodium management is important for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for yam talay as a composite dish in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries using weighted proportions typical of a Thai seafood salad recipe. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database reference range for low-carbohydrate seafood salad composites. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-based estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked shrimp, squid, mussels, lime juice, fish sauce, and fresh herbs/aromatics. No direct composite entry for yam talay exists in any primary regional approved source. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component entries used for this estimation. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component entries used for this estimation. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in all seafood and plant-based dressing components per USDA SR Legacy — confirmed zero by source data. 5. GI is estimated at 38 based on analogous low-carbohydrate seafood salad composites from the University of Sydney GI Database reference range (30–45 for seafood dishes); no direct yam talay GI measurement exists in the database. 6. The input record used the menu name 'S1 Seafood Caribbean' from Saap Saap Thai - Funan (field survey data); the canonical food name has been normalised to 'Thai Seafood Salad' (yam talay) as the defensible English identity. The original menu name is preserved in aliases. 7. Halal status is listed as true based on the typical preparation of yam talay without pork or alcohol; individual restaurant preparation should be verified. 8. FoodOn URI FOODON_03302114 and FoodEx2 code A0CKP are used as closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code for yam talay exists in either system.
